name: Web Registries
description: Allow creation of custom registries, similar to customElementsRegistry, for many purposes.

name: Web Injectors
description: Dependency injection system for the web. Supports injector, provide/consume syntax.
details: The Web Injectors project aims to bring standard dependency injection to the web platform, solving the problem of loading dependencies without build tools or import maps limitations. It introduces injector, provide, and consume syntax to managing isolated contexts and explicit data flow.

name: Web Resources
description: Standard protocols for data exchange, interceptors, and resource definition.

name: Web Components
description: Enhanced implementation of Custom Elements supporting Scoped Registries and standard Dependency Injection.

name: Web Behaviors
description: Standard to allow custom attributes to add functionality to any HTML element.

name: Web Contexts
description: Protocol enabling hierarchical data flow and service provision across component trees, effectively solving prop-drilling natively.

name: Web States
description: Standard primitives for observability and state management (signals, stores, and schemas).

name: Web Expressions
description: Parser-agnostic infrastructure for dynamic content interpolation in HTML, supporting custom template syntax.

name: Web Directives
description: Structural directives (loops, conditionals), comment markers, and template extensions for powerful DOM logic.
details: Extends HTML with structural controls like loops and conditionals. Includes 'Comment Directives' for attaching logic without wrapper elements (formerly Web Figments) and 'Template Directives' for advanced content projection.

name: Web Events
description: Scoped event discrimination for cross-team DOM events. Extends addEventListener with provider-based instanceof validation.
details: Web Events solves the DOM event collision problem: when nested components fire identically-named events, listeners have no structural way to distinguish them. The project introduces listenForScoped — a single API that resolves the expected event class from the injector scope of event.target and validates via instanceof. In plugged mode, this becomes a provider option on standard addEventListener.

name: Web Blocks
description: Standard protocols for interoperable modules (components, behaviors) ensuring consistent UX and accessibility across libraries.

name: Web Plugs
description: The missing browser standards (polyfilled). Runs 'plugged' (native patches) or 'unplugged' (safe libs) as the foundation for Web Blocks.

name: Web Intents
description: Declarative profiles for application-wide UX/UI behavior (e.g., density, motion, interaction models) that components automatically adhere to.

name: Web Analytics
description: Standard for vendor-neutral tracking (Identify, Track, Page) using the Segment Spec vocabulary.

name: Web Traces
description: Standard for execution flow monitoring and distributed tracing, aligning with W3C Trace Context and OpenTelemetry.

name: Web Portals
description: Mimics React portals: render an element outside its logical parent in the DOM, but retain its web contexts. Enables overlays, modals, and tooltips to visually escape their parent while staying connected to their logical ancestors' data and services.

name: Web Positioning
description: Standard for anchoring a floating element to another element (tooltips, popovers, menus): placement, collision handling, and tethering. A single positioning provider that any component delegates to — native-first (CSS Anchor Positioning) with pluggable adapters (Floating UI) — so components from different vendors share one engine instead of bundling ten.

name: Web Reliability
description: Standard for handling mechanism failures — retries, recovery strategies, and resilience patterns — across any operation (HTTP, database, computation, async tasks). A registry of recovery handlers tried in priority order; the first that accepts the error owns the recovery. Distinct from validation: reliability handles system failures, not input invalidity.

name: Web Validation
description: Standard for input invalidity across form controls, groups, forms, and nested wizards. Defines the Validation protocol — observable states/events per level, merged validity from independent sources, commitment strategies, async resolution registries — so implementations from different vendors are swappable and combinable. Distinct from reliability: validation handles user data; reliability handles mechanism failure.

openQuestions.question: When validity merges from independent sources, is it strictest-wins or last-writer-wins?
Does a custom form control built on ElementInternals get :user-invalid for free, or must the standard re-expose touched/dirty state?

name: Web Intl
description: Standard for localization — turning one UI into many locales: message formatting (Unicode MessageFormat 2.0), locale negotiation and fallback chains, lazy translation loading, and RTL/bidi adaptation. Native Intl and CSS logical properties are the default; FormatJS / i18next / Lingui plug in behind a translation registry. Defines the Localization protocol so message catalogs and locale strategies from different vendors are swappable and combinable.

name: Web Traits
description: Standard for creating granular, auto-loadable features that bridge the gap between easy developer experience and optimal performance.

name: Web Adapters
description: Bridges and syntactic sugars connecting external libraries or languages to Web Everything protocols.

name: Web Cases
description: A collection of isolated HTML test cases serving as the source of truth for protocol conformity and documentation examples.

name: Web Manifests
description: Standard for defining project metadata, dependencies, and capabilities in a framework-agnostic way.

name: Web Docs
description: The meta-standard that orchestrates Web Manifests and Web Cases to generate comprehensive documentation sites.

name: Web Guards
description: Standard for guarding a region's lifecycle against a predicate: a policy attached to a region (document, route, modal, element), evaluated at a lifecycle event, whose deny-branch resolves to a member-specific outcome. The predicate is resolved by a swappable guard provider (native-first default → project override → custom plug), so swapping a policy engine leaves the guarded surface untouched. Generalizes navigation/exit guards (unsaved-state) and access control (authorization) onto one provider seam.

name: Web Lifecycle
description: Standard for a domain entity's lifecycle: a declared status set with guarded, role-scoped transitions and a canonical status presentation. A loan, a policy, a claim, an order, a ticket — each is a state machine (draft → submitted → … → closed) whose legal moves depend on the actor and on guard predicates. The transition map is declarative; whether an actor may fire a transition is delegated to a guard provider (Web Guards), and each transition is an observable event other standards (audit, reporting) compose on. Distinct from Web States (reactive state primitives) and from a region's lifecycle (Web Guards): this is the domain object's workflow state.

name: Web Audit
description: Standard for an immutable, actor-attributed history of consequential actions on a domain entity — who did what, when, and before→after. Every state transition, cleared condition, accepted/rejected document, and issued decision is recorded append-only and surfaced as a queryable timeline. It fixes the AuditEvent schema and a swappable audit provider (in-memory, event-sourced, or an OSCAL backend behind one seam), and composes: a lifecycle (Web Lifecycle) transition becomes one audit event automatically. Distinct from Web States change-tracking (reactive, transient mutation observation) and from Web Compliance's enforcement-evidence trail (the platform twin, same schema, different subject): this is the domain object's operational record.

name: Web Decisions
description: Standard for an explainable, reproducible decision record — why a rules/criteria evaluation reached its outcome. For each criterion: its input, operator, threshold, and the outcome it contributed, aggregated into a finding against a versioned ruleset the record carries so the explanation replays. A schema-only interchange contract: the DecisionRecord shape is the lock (no provider) — an underwriting engine, an eligibility engine, a pricing or moderation engine all emit the same record, so Web Reporting and Web Compliance consume any of them and audit-trail logs each as one event. Distinct from the rule engine + proof machinery (proof-of-compliance, #093), from Web Validation (form-field invalidity), and from Web Traces (execution spans): this is the decision's explanation artifact.

name: Web Theme
description: Standard for the concrete-value layer of a design system — the primitive scales (spacing, radius, elevation, type ramp, color palette) and per-component overrides that the semantic Web Intents (density, surface, typography, motion, theme-color) resolve into. Authored and interchanged as DTCG 2025.10 (the stable W3C design-token format — the one legitimate Protocol here, adopted not coined) and compiled to the native CSS runtime: custom properties + @property, with light-dark()/color-scheme for schemes and relative-color/color-mix() for derived accent scales, validated against WCAG/APCA per step. Ships a complete default token set; a consuming project overrides via extends rather than authoring from scratch. A 3-tier taxonomy (primitive · semantic · component) where the semantic tier stays owned by the intents — no parallel vocabulary. Distinct from Web Intents (the UX-“what”): Web Theme supplies the values that “what” points into.

name: Web Policy
description: Standard for declarative domain policy — the rules that govern whether an action is allowed, an entity qualifies, or an approval is required (“orders over $X need sign-off”, “a minor can't consent”). Distinct from Web Validation (input invalidity — a field is wrong): policy decides whether an action is permitted, a different concern with its own home. Adopts the OMG DMN vocabulary — decision tables + FEEL-style expressions — as the rule meta-schema (standardize the meta-schema, not the rule list), with rule versioning and per-context/tenant scoping. Composes the existing substrate — Web Expressions (conditional logic), Web Contexts (scoped policy flow), Web Validation, Web Traces (the proof point) — rather than absorbing into them. Rule authoring surfaces as a Technical Configurator domain (plateau-app). Foundation for the proof-of-compliance (tamper-evident decision log) and enforcement (PDP/PEP) slices.

name: Web Reporting
description: Standard for a unified report substrate — one normalized model (sources, sections, findings, severities, scores, series) every producer targets so reports from different sources render through the same UI, with adapters that ingest (and emit) common report formats instead of bespoke viewers. Defines the report-model Protocol: multi-vendor report-format interop (SARIF, JUnit, coverage, SonarQube all exchange the same kind of artifact) is a genuine, escapable, impl-swappable contract — the normalized schema is the lock, and ingest/export adapters bridge each foreign format bottom-up into the pivot. A Project that owns one Protocol; an intent would be UX-only and wrong for a data-interchange contract. Distinct from a catalog page (which renders a registry): reporting renders findings/results over time. The output layer the conformance tooling, a future Web Compliance project, and external sources all render through.

name: Web Compliance
description: Standard for the enforcement layer — deliberately separated from conformance. Conformance is how aligned something is to a standard (a measure on a spectrum — it reports, it doesn't block); compliance is a conformance criterion promoted to an enforced hard rule — a gate, with severity, pass/fail, expiring waivers, and an audit trail. Web Compliance owns that promotion path and its machinery. The canonical artifact is the policy/rule model: which conformance criteria are enforced, at what severity, for which scope — policy-as-code, versioned, that extends a fully-defined platform-default policy (a project policy extends/overrides the baseline, per the WE config-layer convention). No compliance-specific protocol in v1: results ride Web Reporting's report model (an audit result is just another report source). Distinct from Web Policy (declarative domain rules — whether a business action is permitted): Web Compliance gates standards conformance, a different subject with its own home.

name: Web Notifications
description: Standard for the notification domain — the orchestration concern (which surface a notification targets, grouping, dedup, badging) plus the closed-app push-delivery protocol. Ratified in #009 (domain home) and #455 (transport home, by-purpose: push lives here, the open-app realtime family gets its own home). Push delivery is a protocol, not a UX intent: subscription + VAPID + provider orchestration is async, server-authoritative, and swappable — the native anchor is the Web Push API (service worker + push service, survives tab close), with a CustomPushProvider seam so a multi-channel hub (FCM, OneSignal, Novu) plugs in behind the same contract. It coordinates the render-intent family (system-notification, notification-marker) which publish standalone in Web Intents — a consuming project sees the render intents, never the transport. Distinct from feedback (in-app toast render) and background-task (in-session persistence): this owns the OS-surfaced, server-deliverable notification.

name: Web Realtime
description: Standard for the open-app realtime transport — the live, bidirectional-or-streamed server→client delivery channel an open app holds (presence, live data, collaborative cursors, server-pushed updates). Defines the transport-negotiation Protocol: a CustomTransportProvider seam that auto-negotiates the best available channel — SSE and WebSocket native-first, WebTransport capability-gated (Chrome/Edge only, no Safari — graceful degradation), long-poll/poll as the degradation floor — so a consuming app declares realtime delivery and the provider picks the transport, the way Socket.IO / SignalR / Centrifugo auto-negotiate. Ratified in #455 (Fork 2, transport home by purpose). Deliberately separated from Web Notifications’ closed-app push: the two are non-fungible — a live socket and a service-worker push survive opposite lifecycles (open tab vs. closed app) and you never fall back from one to the other. Technical contract, not a UX preference — a Protocol, not an Intent.
